finding: §3.2 replaced round 1's open-ended (ellipsis) writer list with an explicit, closed enumeration — "every command that acquires the task-file write lock and rewrites the file — currently tp done, tp close, tp set, tp reopen, tp remove, tp add, tp claim, tp commit, and tp import" — but omits `tp next`, which claims the next ready task (open->wip) and rewrites the file via model.WriteTaskFile. §10.2's round-trip acceptance list omits `tp next` too. As a primary incremental command that mutates the task file, its write path must preserve the sparse `workflow` block, yet it is neither enumerated in the invariant nor exercised by any acceptance test, so a re-materialization regression on the claim path (the exact bug this release fixes) could ship untested.
suggestion: Add `tp next` to §3.2's enumerated writer set and to §10.2's round-trip operation list (a tp init-created `{}` block, and a `{"review_max_rounds":4}` block, must survive `tp next` claiming a task with the workflow field set unchanged).

finding: The precedence chain in §1 names the second tier '`TP_FILE` env' (CLI flag > `TP_FILE` env > task-file override > project > built-in), but TP_FILE is tp's task-file *selector* env var (Task File Discovery), not a workflow-*value* source. §3.4 and §4.2 both state the same chain with a generic 'env' tier, and §3.4's `tp config --resolved` label set includes `source: "env"` — a value-origin label that TP_FILE can never satisfy (TP_FILE only picks which file populates the *task* tier, which §1 lists separately and below it). §1 therefore conflates file discovery with value resolution and mismatches the authoritative resolution sections it is meant to summarize.
suggestion: In §1, replace '`TP_FILE` env' with the generic 'env' tier to match §3.4/§4.2 and the `source: "env"` label, or drop the TP_FILE parenthetical entirely so the env value-tier is not mislabeled as the file-selector variable.

finding: §4.1 labels `quality_gate` and `commit_strategy` "presence-tracked strings" and asserts the override "represents every task-file workflow field, so none is dropped"; §3.2 promises round-trip leaves the set of present fields unchanged. But unlike the integers (pointer fields, §3.5) and `checks` (pointer-to-slice, §4.3), no section pins how an explicit empty string is kept distinct from absence for these two string fields. The obvious mechanism — `omitempty` on a plain string (the representation these fields already use) — cannot preserve a present empty value: an authored or `tp set` `quality_gate: ""` (present, meaning "no gate") serializes identically to absent and is silently dropped on the next write, contradicting §3.2's "present fields unchanged" and §4.1's "none is dropped". §4.3 spells out the empty-vs-absent rule for checks but there is no parallel rule telling the implementer whether an explicit empty quality_gate/commit_strategy is a meaningful "no gate / no strategy" override or is normalized to absence.
suggestion: State explicitly whether an empty-string quality_gate/commit_strategy is (a) a meaningful present override — which requires *string presence tracking mirroring §4.3's checks — or (b) normalized to absence; if (b), call out the round-trip exception so §3.2's "present fields unchanged" is not contradicted for the empty-string case.

finding: §6.1 asserts an out-of-range value is rejected with exit code 1 for tp set --workflow, but pins concrete valid ranges for only two of the five integer fields: review_clean_rounds (1–10) and gate_timeout_seconds (30–3600). The ranges for audit_clean_rounds, review_max_rounds, and audit_max_rounds are never stated anywhere in the spec, and §3.5 notes review_max_rounds: 0 is a VALID no-cap value while review_clean_rounds: 0 is INVALID — so the boundaries genuinely differ per field. A QA engineer cannot write deterministic accept/reject boundary tests (e.g. audit_clean_rounds=0, review_max_rounds=51) from the spec alone; two engineers would guess different limits.
suggestion: State the valid range for all five integer workflow fields (or explicitly reference the v0.24.0 range definitions and note they are unchanged), and add an acceptance case in §10.4 for at least one non-review_clean_rounds field so the general rejection claim is testable.

finding: §1 (line 5) writes the workflow-value precedence as 'CLI flag > TP_FILE env > task-file workflow override > .tp/config.json > built-in', naming TP_FILE as the env layer. But TP_FILE is the task-FILE-path discovery env var (--file > TP_FILE > .tp/local.json), not a source of workflow VALUES; no env var that supplies a workflow value is named here. §3.4 and §4.2 use a bare 'env' with no concrete var. A tester cannot write an 'env layer beats task layer' resolution test because no workflow-value env var is defined, and the one env var that is named (TP_FILE) demonstrably carries no workflow value.
suggestion: Either name the actual env var(s) that feed a workflow value at the env layer, or drop the 'TP_FILE' qualifier and state that the env layer for workflow values is inherited unchanged from v0.24.0 (with a pointer to where it is defined), so the env-layer precedence is verifiable.
